initial_claims.location: lendGovernmentMoney() crash reset block - creditorAddresses = new address[](0) and creditorAmounts = new uint[](0)
initial_claims.description: In Solidity 0.4, reassigning a storage array with `array = new T[](0)` zeroes every existing slot in storage one by one. As creditorAddresses and creditorAmounts grow with each lend call, the gas cost of the reset operation grows linearly. Once the arrays are large enough, the reset block will exceed the block gas limit, making it impossible to ever trigger the crash/reset path. This permanently locks funds in the contract and prevents the Ponzi from ever paying out the last creditor or the corruptElite on crash.
initial_claims.evidence: Lines: `creditorAddresses = new address[](0);` and `creditorAmounts = new uint[](0);` inside the `if (lastTimeOfNewCredit + TWELVE_HOURS < block.timestamp)` branch. Each call to lendGovernmentMoney with amount >= 1 ETH pushes one entry to each array. After enough entries (~hundreds to low thousands depending on block gas limit), the reset will OOG.

claim.location: lendGovernmentMoney() crash reset block - creditorAddresses = new address[](0) and creditorAmounts = new uint[](0)
claim.description: In Solidity 0.4, reassigning a storage array with `array = new T[](0)` zeroes every existing slot in storage one by one. As creditorAddresses and creditorAmounts grow with each lend call, the gas cost of the reset operation grows linearly. claim.evidence: Lines: `creditorAddresses = new address[](0);` and `creditorAmounts = new uint[](0);` inside the `if (lastTimeOfNewCredit + TWELVE_HOURS < block.timestamp)` branch. Each call to lendGovernmentMoney with amount >= 1 ETH pushes one entry to each array. After enough entries (~hundreds to low thousands depending on block gas limit), the reset will OOG.

verdict.reasoning: The vulnerable pattern exists at the cited location. In the crash/reset branch of `lendGovernmentMoney()`, the code executes `creditorAddresses = new address[](0)` and `creditorAmounts = new uint[](0)`. In Solidity 0.4.x, reassigning a storage array to a new empty array zeroes out all existing storage slots one by one (SSTORE for each element), costing 5,000 gas per slot for zeroing (or 20,000 for non-zero to zero in older EVM). Each call to `lendGovernmentMoney()` with >= 1 ETH pushes one entry to each array. After enough entries accumulate, the gas cost of zeroing both arrays in the reset block will exceed the block gas limit (which was ~4-5M gas at the time of this contract's deployment in 2016). This makes it physically impossible to execute the crash reset path, permanently locking funds in the contract. The last creditor and corruptElite can never receive their crash payouts. No array size cap, no gas check, and no alternative reset mechanism exists in the code. The Defender correctly acknowledges there is no mitigation. The harm falls on creditors who deposited ETH expecting eventual payout — their funds become permanently locked. The attacker can deliberately spam small (1 ETH) deposits to grow the arrays to the critical size, then wait 12 hours, at which point no one can trigger the reset. This is a realistic, achievable attack on mainnet.
verdict.recommendation: Replace the storage array reassignment with a pattern that does not require zeroing all slots: (1) Use a mapping with an index counter instead of dynamic arrays, and simply increment the round counter to invalidate old entries. (2) Or delete arrays element by element in a loop with a gas limit check, allowing partial resets across multiple transactions. (3) Or use a 'generation' counter so old array entries are logically invalidated without storage zeroing. The fundamental fix is to avoid O(n) storage writes in a single transaction where n is unbounded.

There is no mitigation in the code that prevents this attack. The arrays grow unboundedly with each call to lendGovernmentMoney() that includes >= 1 ETH, and the reset block unconditionally attempts to zero both arrays via storage reassignment. No gas limit checks, no array size caps, and no alternative reset mechanism exist.

The vulnerable pattern exists at the cited location. In the crash/reset branch of `lendGovernmentMoney()`, the code executes `creditorAddresses = new address[](0)` and `creditorAmounts = new uint[](0)`. Each call to `lendGovernmentMoney()` with >= 1 ETH pushes one entry to each array. After enough entries accumulate, the gas cost of zeroing both arrays in the reset block will exceed the block gas limit (which was ~4-5M gas at the time of this contract's deployment in 2016). This makes it physically impossible to execute the crash reset path, permanently locking funds in the contract. The last creditor and corruptElite can never receive their crash payouts. No array size cap, no gas check, and no alternative reset mechanism exists in the code. The Defender correctly acknowledges there is no mitigation. The harm falls on creditors who deposited ETH expecting eventual payout — their funds become permanently locked. The attacker can deliberately spam small (1 ETH) deposits to grow the arrays to the critical size, then wait 12 hours, at which point no one can trigger the reset.
